coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Citrus limetta peels: Exploration of pH sensing, non-hemolytic activity and thermoluminescent properties
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: González-Martínez, Gerardo A.
García, L.G.
Frutis-Murillo, Minerva
Martínez-Torres, Pablo
López-Meza, Joel E
Rosas, G.
coredata.dc:description: This study introduces a novel hydrothermal green method for synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using dried powders of Citrus limetta peels as a carbon source. The synthesized CQDs were evaluated for their photothermal response, pH sensing under various conditions and biocompatibility through non-hemolytic activity. CQDs had low crystallinity and an average size of 3 nm, ranging from 1 to 6 nm. CQDs have biocompatible properties, which makes them suitable for developing pH sensors. The study also revealed that the temperature of CQDs increases up to 37 °C under laser light irradiation of 808 nm, which might have potential applications in the biomedical field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Hydrothermal synthesis
pH sensing
Thermoluminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis spectroscopy of the CQDs as a function of (a) temperature of hydrothermal reaction decomposition and (b) hydrothermal decomposition time.
A) effect of ph on the fluorescence intensity of the cqds, and b) hemolysis assay at different concentrations of cqds.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Citrus limetta peels: Exploration of pH sensing, non-hemolytic activity and thermoluminescent properties

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study introduces a novel hydrothermal green method for synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using dried powders of Citrus limetta peels as a carbon source. The synthesized CQDs were evaluated for their photothermal response, pH sensing under various conditions and biocompatibility through non-hemolytic activity. CQDs had low crystallinity and an average size of 3 nm, ranging from 1 to 6 nm. CQDs have biocompatible properties, which makes them suitable for developing pH sensors. The study also revealed that the temperature of CQDs increases up to 37 °C under laser light irradiation of 808 nm, which might have potential applications in the biomedical field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: CQDs have emerged as a fascinating class of nanomaterials, distinguished by their unique optical, electronic, and chemical properties [1]. Their ability to emit light makes them invaluable fluorescent probes for bioimaging, nanomedicine [2], and sensing applications [3]. Moreover, their excellent load-carrying properties make them as promising candidates for electronic and photovoltaic devices [4]. CQDs, with sizes ranging from 1 to 10 nm and a quasi-spherical morphology, contain sp2 hybridized carbon atoms and occasionally sp3 hybridized atoms [5]. Hydrothermal treatment of plant species at high temperatures, is gaining attention in the scientific community [6]. Table S1 shows revision of the recent green synthesis of CQDs. In addition, the photothermal capacity of CQDs has been explored through various radiation sources [7].

This study introduces a novel hydrothermal green method for producing CQDs utilizing dry powders of Citrus limetta peels and polyethylene glycol (PEG) as an additional solvent. The synthesized CQDs were evaluated for their photothermal response and pH sensing capabilities under various conditions. Additionally, their biocompatibility was assessed through a hemolysis assay.

Citrus limetta peel was cleaned, dried and crushed into a uniform powder. Specifically, 0.21 g of dried peel, 4 mL of PEG, and 1 mL of deionized water were placed in an autoclave and heated under controlled temperatures (170–185 °C) for 8 and 24 h. The CQDs were characterized using various techniques, including UV–Vis spectroscopy (VELAB VE-5100UV), X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advanced DAVINCI), infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker Tensor 27), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100), photothermal analysis (laser with a wavelength of 808 nm, TESTO 875 thermal camera) and photoluminescence (PL) emission evaluation (Varioskan™ LUX multimode microplate reader).

5 µL of CQDs dissolved in 100 µL deionized water were used and buffered with pH from 4 to 11 for pH detection, with an emission evaluated at 350 nm using the same microplate reader. The hemolysis test was also evaluated using a 1 % mixture of erythrocytes mixed with phosphate-based saline pH 7 and various concentrations of CQDs (6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 50, 100 µg/mL) incubated for 1 h at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 and constant humidity. Finally, the absorbance reading at 405 nm Triton 10 % was used as a positive control for hemolysis.

XRD patterns of the reference commercial carbon and the CQDs are shown in Fig. S1a-b. displays a single sharp peak belongs to a carbon hexagonal structure with a spacing of d = 0.396 nm for the (002) planes. In contrast, Fig. S1b displays a broad diffraction peak indicating the presence of functionalized CQDs. The broad peak suggests that the CQDs have small size and low crystallinity, which is consistent with other works [8].

Fig. S1c shows a bright-field TEM micrograph of the CQDs prepared with the Citrus limetta peels at 185 °C for 24 h. The CQDs appear dispersed with an average particle size of 3 nm, ranging from 1 to 6 nm. The high-resolution TEM analysis revealed poor crystallinity of the CQDs, as shown in Fig. S1d-e, possibly due to the extended pressure applied to the material during the synthesis. However, some CQDs show lattice fringes of 0.32 nm corresponding to the interplanar distances of the (002) planes of the hexagonal graphite. XRD and TEM results prove that Citrus limetta peels can be used to synthesize CQDs.

Fig. 1 a displays the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CQDs synthesized at different temperatures (185, 180, and 170 °C). The spectra exhibit two absorption bands at 210 nm and 280 nm attributed to the π-π* transition of C=C and C–C bonds, respectively, involving aromatic sp2 carbons from the CQDs. The peak at 280 nm is assigned to the n-π* transition of the C=O groups, indicating their successful oxidation and functionalization. Then, CQDs have the typical strong optical absorption in the UV region, which characterizes their photoluminescence behavior, effectively collecting photons in the short wavelength region. Additionally, the optical absorption of CQDs increased as the temperature rose from 170 to 185 °C, displaying a blue shift that indicates a reduction in their size. As the temperature increases, more organic matter decomposes, promoting CQDs nucleation, resulting in a larger quantity of CQDs formed and, consequently, a decrease in particle size. Given the previous results, the sample was selected for subsequent measurements at 185 °C and 24 h of reaction time.

Fig. 1b shows that the intensity of the bands at 8 h was lower than those obtained at 24 h, revealing that there were still undecomposed residues of the carbon source for a short time of the procedure, progressively eliminated up to 24 h. The inset in Fig. 1b shows CQDs luminescent under UV radiation after 24 h of hydrothermal treatment, demonstrating their successful formation using the Citrus limetta as a carbon source. Citrus limetta was a sustainable, profitable, and eco-friendly raw material that minimized toxic waste production in CQDs. The luminescence of the CQDs colloidal solution is an important characteristic of these nanomaterials.

Our research on Citrus limetta made a unique contribution to CQDs synthesis. Fig. 2 presents the PL spectra conducted to prove the optical properties of the CQDs. The excitation wavelengths ranged from 300 to 475 nm, and the highest emission intensity was observed at 450 nm with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. These results confirm the quantum fluorescence effect of CQDs synthesized using the Citrus limetta peels.

FTIR analysis (Fig. S2) presents the functional groups of the PEG (red line) used as a solvent and the CQDs (black line). Both spectrums present absorption bands: O–H, –COOH, C–H C-O, and C-O-C. However, the band located at 1640 cm-1 of the carbonyl group C=O is responsible for stabilizing the CQDs.

The fluorescence suitability of CQDs as biomarkers was assessed across varying pH levels (pH 4–11), as shown in Fig. 3 a. The pH in physiological conditions is important for controlling and diagnosing different diseases. If the pH is below 7.40, it can result in cardio-pulmonary and neurological problems. If the pH value becomes more alkaline (7–9), it indicates the presence of bacteria.

A in vitro hemolysis assay served as a simple and reliable method to ascertain CQDs' biocompatibility. Previous findings indicate that biomaterials with hemolysis rates below 5 % are deemed acceptable [9]. Fig. 3b illustrates that different CQDs concentrations, with variances not exceeding 5 %, exhibit potential for practical applications like biosensors and bioimaging. This versatility stems from their demonstrated biocompatibility and stability across different pH conditions, which are the best at pH 8.

Fig. 4 shows the thermograms and photothermal images of the different experiments carried out to evaluate the energy conversion of the CQDs. The thermograms corresponding to PEG (c) and PEG-H2O (d) used as references do not significantly increase the temperature (2 °C and 1.5 °C). In the corresponding photothermal images, a color change from blue to red is expected as the temperature increases; these images are captured in the infrared spectrum. However, no appreciable change was presented in either of the previous experiments.

Nevertheless, the thermograms recorded for CQDs obtained at 185 °C for 8 h show an increase in the temperature of the solution around 9 °C. Also, the photothermal image inset turns medium red, indicating an internal temperature increase. When the CQDs temperature of the reaction decomposition was 185 °C for 24 h, the internal temperature recorded increased up to 39 °C, as seen in the graph. At the same time, the photothermal image changes in color from blue to intense red generated by infrared radiation according to the increase in temperature. The increment in solution temperature is attributed to the heat flow generated by the excitation and decay of the CQDs valence electrons, characterized by UV–Vis and PL spectroscopic techniques. Also, it confirms that the largest number of CQDs was obtained at 185 °C for 24 h, which raises the light conversion to heat in a prolonged action. This result confirms that CQDs transform light to heat of 37 °C by subtracting the heat released by the reference substances. This result is promising for using CQDs to convert solar energy for energy conversion or biomedical applications such as drug delivery detection or therapy.

CQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using dried Citrus limetta peels as a carbon source. The results indicated CQDs with low crystallinity and an average size of 3 nm ranging from 1 to 6 nm. CQDs are biocompatible and can be used as pH sensors. It was determined that the internal temperature of CQDs increases up to 37 °C under light irradiation of 808 nm, which may be useful for their biomedical applications.

Gerardo A. González-Martínez: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. L.G. García: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Minerva Frutis-Murillo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Pablo Martínez-Torres: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Joel E López-Meza: Writing – review & editing, Resources. G. Rosas: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2024.137119.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots: Synthesis, properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, A. Hu, Carbon quantum dots: Synthesis, properties and applications, J Mater Chem C 2 (2014) 6921–6939. https://doi.org/10.1039/c4tc00988f.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.T. Zheng, A. Ananthanarayanan, K.Q. Luo, P. Chen, Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: Properties, syntheses, and biological applications, Small 11 (2015) 1620–1636. https://doi.org/10.1002/smll.201402648.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Principles, mechanisms, and application of carbon quantum dots in sensors: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.J. Molaei, Principles, mechanisms, and application of carbon quantum dots in sensors: A review, Analytical Methods 12 (2020) 1266–1287. https://doi.org/10.1039/c9ay02696g.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-based quantum dots for photovoltaic devices: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Kim, J.K. Dash, P. Kumar, R. Patel, Carbon-Based Quantum Dots for Photovoltaic Devices: A Review, ACS Appl Electron Mater 4 (2022) 27–58. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsaelm.1c00783.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: sp2–sp 3 -hybridized atomic domains determine optical features of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. V. Tepliakov, E. V. Kundelev, P.D. Khavlyuk, Y. Xiong, M.Yu. Leonov, W. Zhu, A. V. Baranov, A. V. Fedorov, A.L. Rogach, I.D. Rukhlenko, sp2–sp 3 -Hybridized Atomic Domains Determine Optical Features of Carbon Dots, ACS Nano 13 (2019) 10737–10744. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsnano.9b05444.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in the synthesis of carbon dots from renewable biomass by high-efficient hydrothermal and microwave green approaches

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Bressi, A.M. Balu, D. Iannazzo, C. Espro, Recent advances in the synthesis of carbon dots from renewable biomass by high-efficient hydrothermal and microwave green approaches, Curr Opin Green Sustain Chem 40 (2023) 100742. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cogsc.2022.100742.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photothermal effect of carbon quantum dots enhanced photoelectrochemical water splitting of hematite photoanodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Hu, J. Huang, F. Zhao, P. Yi, B. He, Y. Wang, T. Chen, Y. Chen, Z. Li, X. Liu, Photothermal effect of carbon quantum dots enhanced photoelectrochemical water splitting of hematite photoanodes, J Mater Chem A 8 (2020) 14915–14920. https://doi.org/10.1039/d0ta04144k.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications in photocatalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Wang, K.-Q. Lu, Z.-R. Tang, Y.-J. Xu, Recent progress in carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications in photocatalysis, J Mater Chem A 5 (2017) 3717–3734. https://doi.org/10.1039/C6TA08660H.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dot induced hemolysis and anti-angiogenesis in proliferating cancers with Vitis vinifera as the source material

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.R. Parvathy, P.K. Praseetha, Carbon quantum dot induced hemolysis and anti-angiogenesis in proliferating cancers with Vitis vinifera as the source material, Vegetos 36 (2022) 890–898. https://doi.org/10.1007/s42535-022-00454-8
